title: Experimental observation of strong field stabilization
authors: Anna R. Dardia, Spencer Walker, Yifei Bai, Petros Kousis, Alexandra S. Landsman, David M. Weld
abstract: Strong oscillating fields are expected to tear apart bound quantum states. Theoretical studies predict a striking reversal: that as the field intensity is raised above some threshold, bound states like atoms can become increasingly stable, accompanied by a spatial bifurcation of the bound state wavefunction. This strong field stabilization was predicted decades ago in the context of atoms in pulsed laser fields, but has resisted experimental observation due to extreme intensity requirements and theoretical controversy. Here we report the experimental observation of strong-field stabilization of a ground state, using trapped neutral atoms to emulate the dynamics of bound electrons in an extremely strong laser field. We image the predicted wavepacket bifurcation, measure an ionization rate non-monotonic in field amplitude, and map out the regime of stabilization. Stabilization persists down to surprisingly low drive frequencies, on the order of the lowest-energy excitations. These results confirm a long-standing prediction in extreme quantum dynamics, and showcase a complementary tool for probing strong-field phenomena near and beyond the frontier of current laser technology.
summary: This research reports the first experimental observation of strong-field stabilization in trapped atoms, confirming a decades-old prediction that intense laser fields can stabilize quantum states. By measuring ionization rates and observing wavepacket bifurcation, the authors validate the theory using ultracold atoms as an analogue for bound electrons. This work advances the understanding of quantum dynamics in extreme electromagnetic environments.
main_problem: The paper investigates the counter-intuitive phenomenon of strong-field stabilization, where intense oscillating fields stabilize bound quantum states against ionization.
main_result: The authors experimentally observe strong-field stabilization in trapped atoms and confirm the predicted wavefunction bifurcation, showing stabilization persists even at surprisingly low drive frequencies.
method: The study combines experimental measurements on trapped ultracold atoms with detailed numerical simulations using the Gross-Pitaevskii Equation (GPE) to model the dynamics.
model_or_system: The system uses trapped neutral atoms (e.g., Sr BEC) subjected to time-dependent inertial forces to emulate the dynamics of bound electrons in an intense laser field, modeled by the Kramers-Henneberger potential.
key_observables: Ionization rate (loss rate), spatial bifurcation/dichotomy of the wavepacket, and surviving population fraction as a function of field amplitude.
important_parameters: Quiver amplitude ($A$), drive frequency ($\omega$), and the critical amplitude ($A_{	ext{crit}}$) required to induce stabilization.
main_assumptions: The single-active electron approximation is used in some analyses, and the GPE simulations are noted to neglect finite-temperature or beyond-mean-field effects.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the experimental setup, sub-cycle dynamics, wavepacket bifurcation, and quantitative plots showing the non-monotonic dependence of the ionization rate on the quiver amplitude, confirming stabilization.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the theoretical prediction of stabilization, detailing the experimental platform using trapped atoms, presenting measurements of ionization rates and wavepacket bifurcation across varying field parameters, and concluding with a general confirmation of the phenomenon's robustness.
why_interesting: This work provides a crucial experimental realization of a long-standing theoretical prediction in extreme quantum dynamics, offering a complementary tool to probe strong-field physics inaccessible with current pulsed laser technology.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Probing strong-field dynamics with trapped atoms. a) Diagram of the experimental setup. The trap position is varied by an acousto-optic modulator to induce inertial forces which correspond to the electric fields of a laser pulse. b) Absorption images of strontium atoms subjected to a 4-cycle pulse with amplitude A = 38.3 µm and frequency ωdrive = 1.14 ω0 (2π × 32 Hz), taken at various times during the pulse to reveal sub-cycle dynamics. Solid line indicates the time- dependent position of the trap center. The observed unbinding from the trap partway through the pulse corresponds to strong-field photoionization. c) Absorption images of atoms after a 250 ms ramp-up to a range of final...

FIG. 2. Observation of strong-field stabilization. a) Measured unbinding rate in a constant amplitude field of frequency 1.43 ω0 (40 Hz), plotted versus quiver amplitude. After a 6-cycle ramp into a flat-top pulse, the surviving population is measured via absorption imaging after varying durations, and the decay constant is determined by an exponential fit. Note the sharp decrease in loss rate above a critical quiver amplitude. Error bars denote 95% confidence intervals; inset depicts one such fit. b) Surviving population fraction following a 16-cycle 1.43 ω0 (40 Hz) sin2-envelope pulse as a function of quiver amplitude. Symbols denote experimental data; solid red curve shows the results of...

FIG. 3. Dependence of stabilization on frequency and amplitude. a) Normalized surviving population following a 16-cycle pulse with a sin2 envelope following the same procedure as in Fig 2(b), for drive frequencies of 0.89 ω0 to 1.43 ω0 (25 to 40 Hz). Dashed line indicates the point where the quiver amplitude equals w/2. Solid lines in corresponding shades represent the results of GPE numerics. b) 2D stability diagram exploring the dependence of strong-field stabilization on frequency and amplitude over a wider range of amplitudes, in terms of maximum acceleration: Aω2 drive. Each pixel denotes the surviving bound population following a 16-cycle sin2 pulse, normalized by the population in...

FIG. 4. Effect of pulse shape on ionization Measured surviving population versus amplitude following a 16-cycle trapezoidal pulse of frequency 1.34 ω0 (37.5 Hz), with the du- ration of the ramp-up and ramp-down varied. Error bars rep- resent standard error; lines are guides to the eye. Inset shows the trap center motion during a 6-cycle ramp, as measured by a position-sensitive photodiode.

Figure 1: Unshaken Gaussian trap potential (β = 0) as a function of position scaled by the beam waist w. Blue solid: Gaussian potential Vtrap(x) = −V0 exp(−2x2/w2). Magenta dash–dot: cut– parabola approximation (harmonic oscillator truncated at V = 0). Cyan dashed: trapezoidal approximation. The potential depth is V0. Horizontal lines denote the chemical potentials for condensates with N0 = 40 k, 100 k, and 250 k, and the corresponding shaded regions indicate the Thomas–Fermi fillings for each case.
